rationale_for_baseline_tier: This is an acute, uncontained combined emergency: active severe head-banging that has already caused a scalp hematoma and risks traumatic brain injury, physical aggression endangering staff, environmental hazards (broken glass) and a high elopement risk, in a non-verbal patient who cannot be safely approached, assessed, medicated or transported. The source note escalated to 000 for police and ambulance support. Only an immediate, coordinated in-person emergency response (T5) can safely contain the situation, assess and treat the head injury, and begin the search for an underlying painful cause. The patient cannot be safely self-transported, so ambulance plus police assistance is required.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Ongoing or resumed severe head-banging / continued self-inflicted head trauma
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Now — do not wait; the head injury is accumulating in real time
escalation_edges.rationale: Repeated forceful head impacts can cause permanent brain injury or intracranial bleeding; immediate emergency response is required to stop the harm and assess the head.
escalation_edges.communication_template: He is hitting his head hard enough to injure his brain. Call 000 now for an ambulance and police, tell them it is a behavioural emergency with a head injury, and protect his head as safely as you can without putting yourself in reach of biting.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Vomiting, unequal pupils, becoming drowsy or unarousable (serious head-injury / raised intracranial pressure signs) — including after any sedation
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately if any appear; monitor at least every 15 minutes if he becomes sedated
escalation_edges.rationale: These indicate a potentially life-threatening intracranial injury; sedation can mask deterioration, so vigilance must increase, not decrease, after any calming medication.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he vomits, his pupils look uneven, or he becomes hard to wake — even after he calms or is sedated — tell 000 straight away, this can be a serious brain injury.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Uncontained danger to self or others — biting, aggression, broken glass, attempts to abscond
escalation_edges.rationale: A behavioural emergency that endangers staff and cannot be safely contained requires police-assisted emergency response for safe management and transport.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Because he is biting and there is broken glass, ask 000 for police as well as ambulance. Keep a safe distance, move others away, and remove hazards you can reach safely.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Any sign or suspicion of an underlying acute painful/medical cause (e.g. no bowel movement for days, distended abdomen, unable to pass urine, ear/tooth signs, fever)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same emergency assessment — ensure the attending team is explicitly asked to screen for a pain source
escalation_edges.rationale: A treatable painful driver in a non-verbal patient must be found and treated; flag it to the emergency team so behaviour is not merely sedated while the cause is missed.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Tell the paramedics he can't tell us if he's in pain — please ask them to check for things like constipation, a full bladder, an ear or tooth problem, or an infection that could be setting this off.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Non-verbal with profound communication impairment — cannot self-report pain or symptoms
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower the threshold for in-person emergency review and reason from caregiver-observable signs; the communication barrier is an escalation factor, never a reason to wait, and it mandates an active pain/medical screen.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Only two support workers present, managing an aggressive patient amid broken glass
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Staff cannot safely contain, medicate or transport him alone; request police assistance alongside ambulance and prioritise everyone's physical safety.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Profound autism spectrum disorder as the baseline condition
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Do not let the known disability anchor the assessment — an acute change on top of a stable baseline is treated as a new emergency and screened for a medical cause.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 now and ask for both ambulance and police — tell them it is a behavioural emergency in a non-verbal autistic man who is banging his head and has injured it, and who is biting staff. Until they arrive: keep a safe distance, move other people away, clear the broken glass if you can do so safely, protect his head where possible without getting within reach of biting, and do NOT try to force any tablet into his mouth. Tell the paramedics he cannot report pain, so an underlying medical or pain cause needs to be checked for.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Call 000 immediately and request ambulance plus police
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 operates 24 hours a day — this is an emergency and must not wait for in-hours GP or behaviour-team availability.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Provide the emergency and hospital teams with his behaviour support plan and medication list; ensure the GP review the next day reassesses the head injury and follows up the search for and treatment of any underlying painful cause, and that the complex behaviour support team receives a functional-behavioural-assessment referral.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating an acute uncontained behavioural emergency with head trauma as routine/urgent-outpatient behaviour management (e.g. next-day GP review, phone advice to staff), or dismissing it as expected autistic behaviour.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Continued head-banging causes permanent brain injury or a fatal intracranial bleed; a treatable painful cause and staff/patient safety risks go unaddressed.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Recognising an emergency but advising self-transport to ED without police assistance for a patient who is biting and cannot be safely contained or moved.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Over-triage is not a meaningful risk in this case — T5 is the correct tier; the scored failure mode here is under-triage and diagnostic overshadowing.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether an occult acute painful/medical cause is driving the behaviour cannot be resolved remotely because the patient is non-verbal and cannot be examined.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Escalate to emergency in-person assessment and explicitly flag the need to screen for and treat a painful/medical cause; treat 'unlikely on limited assessment' as 'not yet excluded'.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Assuming the behaviour is purely psychological/autistic, ruling out medical mimics from a distance, and under-triaging or attempting remote sedation.
